facade_colour: mixed — commercial ground floor, brick upper

hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: Each property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, each property is associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. The property at 16 Kensington Avenue has value for the modifications made to accommodate a commercial conversion. With its visibly layered built form, the property at 16 Kensington Avenue is also associated with the patterns of commercial adaptation and expansion that began in the early twentieth century. Constructed as pair, the properties have largely retained their original building features including their shared roofline with central gable, first storey bay windows, brick cladding, and fenestration pattern with flat headed openings. Each property has contextual value because it supports and maintains the historic mixed use character of the area and is physically, functionally, visually, and historically linked to the surrounding market area.

context.street_character: One-way SB | 30 km/h | Meets minimum standards | Secondary commercial spine

photo_observations.facade_colour_observed: mixed: blue-painted glass storefront at ground level, brown/red brick upper floor, white-painted wood gable peak
photo_observations.notes: Converted house-form per HCD typology. Commercial ground floor with glass storefront. Upper floor retains Victorian character with original brick and fenestration. Front gable is legible.
photo_observations.facade_condition_notes: Night photo. Ground floor fully converted to commercial with large glass storefront. Upper floor retains original brick with 2 windows. Prominent front-facing gable with white-painted wood peak.
photo_observations.window_details: Ground floor: no traditional windows (full storefront glazing). Second floor: 2 double-hung sash windows with white frames. Gable peak may have small window (hard to see at night).
photo_observations.door_details: Glass storefront door integrated into full-width storefront.
photo_observations.storefront_description: Full-width glass storefront at ground level with blue-tinted interior lighting. Modern commercial conversion of former residential ground floor.

notes: Night photo. Semi-detached house-form with shared roofline and central gable. Commercial conversion at ground level. Brick cladding and flat-headed window openings visible.

The property at 16 Kensington Avenue has value for the modifications made to accommodate a commercial conversion.

facade_description: 16 Kensington Ave presents a house-form, semi-detached facade within the Kensington Market HCD. The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 2 storeys and is capped by a gable, consistent with its house-form, semi-detached typology. The facade reads primarily as mixed — brick cladding with commercial storefront in a mixed — commercial ground floor, brick upper palette, laid in running bond with light grey mortar joints. The facade is organized as a storefront base with 2 upper-window bays, using double-hung sash windows, and 1 principal entrance. HCD note: Each property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, each property is associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. The property at 16 Kensington Avenue has value for the modifications made to accommodate a commercial conversion. With its visibly layered built form, the property at 16 Kensington Avenue is also associated with the patterns of commercial adaptation and expansion that began in the early twentieth century. Constructed as pair, the properties have largely retained their original building features including their shared roofline with central gable, first storey bay windows, brick cladding, and fenestration pattern with flat headed openings. Each property has contextual value because it supports and maintains the historic mixed use character of the area and is physically, functionally, visually, and historically linked to the surrounding market area.
